返回本批次

硅谷已经忘记了普通人想要什么

Silicon Valley has forgotten what normal people want

www.theverge.com 作者 robtherobber 分数 61 评论 42 2026/04/27 21:13
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 认识很多技术人员最令人羞愧的事情之一就是听他们兴奋地告诉我他们认为自己已经做出的一些非常重要的发现。最近，我遇到了一位熟人，他开始对我喋喋不休地谈论他与法学硕士取得的一项惊人发现。事实证明，知识被构造成语言！你可以在 ChatGPT 中输入一个单词，它可能会理解你想要的内容，或者组成一个单词，看看它是否理解你的意思！这些令人惊叹的新工具揭示了英语语料库包含了如此多的关于其使用者的信息！他的结论是法学硕士是与写作同等的发现。大约一个世纪前，普通人就想到了这个想法；对于他告诉我的话，我最慷慨的解释是，他偶然发现了一种天真的、混乱的结构主义版本；索绪尔通过电话游戏。 （最近有一篇关于类似观点的研究，认为人们需要通过文学理论来理解法学硕士，但它是从索绪尔开始的。）我试图尽快退出谈话，尤其是因为他似乎对我没有完全按照他的方式看待事物感到沮丧——这是一种新的行为，很可能是法学硕士过度使用的症状。全身心投入一个未解决的问题需要一定程度的傲慢。但在其他地方，这种傲慢是一种负担。并不是每一个对你来说是新的发现实际上都是新的。例如，埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 对双手的复杂性感到惊叹；我可以指出这是 101 级内容的各种学科：艺术家，他们必须弄清楚如何绘制它们；外科医生，他们必须弄清楚如何对它们进行手术；音乐家和魔术师，他们依靠极其精细的运动技能来创作他们的作品；神经科学家和心理学家，毫无疑问，他们在职业生涯的早期就遇到了皮质侏儒。或者 Palmer Luckey 声称“没有人对“每个孩子一台笔记本电脑”计算项目进行过事后剖析，因为他不知道有一本关于该项目的完整书，名为《魅力机器》。在最荒谬的最低点时，人们会想起 Juicero，一家出售价值 400 美元的榨汁机的公司，它的工作原理与徒手挤压其专有果汁包相同。瞧，发现一些对你来说是新的东西是令人兴奋的——问问任何人听我大喊欧洲（高脂肪）黄油的乐趣 - 但你不能想当然地认为对你来说新的东西对每个人来说都是新的。这些事情都有一个共同点，就是我发现某种技术爱好者普遍缺乏好奇心，尤其是那些对初创企业和创业最感兴趣的人。也许他们是如此孤立，以至于没有意识到他们的“发现”在其他地方是众所周知的，或者也许他们的自我概念是他们是最聪明的，如果他们不知道某件事，那就没有人知道。要全身心投入一个未解决的问题需要一定程度的傲慢——你必须相信自己能够解决它。但在其他地方，这种傲慢是一种负担。它会导致你做出奇怪的事情，比如宣布弗洛伊德发明了内省，而你根本不参与它是一种奖励。在最近的记忆中，制造软件和硬件的人明白他们的工作是为他们的客户服务。当我认为我观察到一些重要的东西时，我的第一个冲动是去图书馆，或者维基百科，或者一个我认为可能知识渊博的人，看看还观察到了什么。例如，当我遭受脑震荡时，我想看看是否有其他人写过关于康复的情况 - 枯燥的医学描述对我来说没有什么帮助。当我无法轻松找到帐户时，我就编写了自己的帐户。多年后，我仍然收到有关此事的电子邮件，这些人在经历了脑震荡后也进行了与我相同的搜索。但做这样的事情需要你理所当然地认为其他人都是聪明的，聪明的人一直存在，人类的经验中很少有什么是新的。你知道，这需要智力上的谦逊，并且愿意考虑他人的经历。虽然这种特殊的傲慢让人感到厌烦，但这不仅仅是一种令人讨厌的个人特质。它似乎也渗透到了硅谷的专业领域。在最近的记忆中，制造软件和硬件的人都明白他们的工作是为客户服务。这是为了确定需求，然后满足它。但在金融危机之后的某个时刻，潜在的企业家开始意识到，他们的工作就是创造未来，而消费者的工作就是顺应所创造的未来。我的猜测是，他们正在模仿史蒂夫·乔布斯（Steve Jobs）所做的事情，例如，当他摆脱了 MacBook Air 上的光驱时。但众所周知，史蒂夫·乔布斯（Steve Jobs）在 20 世纪 80 年代未能发明未来，并被苹果公司开除。我们都知道他回来后事情发生了怎样的变化。但 iMac、iPod、iPhone 都是根据需求而设计的。这iMac 获胜是因为它易于使用。 iPod 比 CD 播放器和一堆 CD 更容易随身携带。 （这也是一种播放您可能非法下载的 MP3 的方式。）iPhone 拥有 App Store，它的实用性远远超出了任何其他移动设备。在某些时候，我们的硅谷霸主忘记了，为了让他们对未来的愿景被采用，人们必须需要它。这在一定程度上是运气——在正确的时间推出了正确的产品。但每种产品都为消费者提供了独特的价值主张。当然，早期采用者会因为这些东西很酷而欣然接受，但不酷的大众并不关心这一点。如果某个东西能以独特的方式改善他们的生活，他们就会购买。 在解决问题的技术方面，公司纷纷追随 NFT、元宇宙和大型语言模型等连续潮流。这些的共同点是它们并不是为了真正解决市场问题而设计的。它们的建立是为了让风险投资家和公司变得富有。 NFT 与加密货币一样，可以让风险投资公司在锁定期缩短的情况下快速抛售投资。虚拟宇宙承诺通过让人们将所有社交活动转移到网上，从而使 Facebook 等公司变得更加丰富，从而可以对其进行监视和货币化。此外，Facebook 的虚拟世界需要购买硬件，然后需要定期升级。在某种程度上，我们的硅谷霸主忘记了，为了让他们对未来的愿景被采用，人们必须想要它。这就是为什么 NFT、元宇宙、Oculus 和 Vision Pro 从未真正找到他们的客户群。诚然，人工智能更有用——例如，它有利于组织大量数据。法学硕士已经得到了消费者的广泛采用，至少只要它们保持免费。但对于法学硕士来说，只有一个真正的客户可以证明建造它们所需的大规模现金焚烧过程是合理的：美国政府。不过，政府合同中只能有少数赢家。所以我们现在看到的是人工智能公司争夺的景象。 OpenAI 也许是最有趣的，因为它试图将自己定位为一种消费品。那些告诉我们人工智能将主宰我们的未来并取代我们工作的人正是希望这会成为现实的人。考虑一下 Sam Altman 告诉世界他需要 ChatGPT 来告诉他如何抚养孩子。你存在。我存在。我们的父母没有法学硕士学位，甚至没有人工智能，但我们不知怎么地度过了童年，就像我们认识的几乎所有成长过程中的人一样，因为几十年来，与人类历史上的大多数其他国家相比，美国的儿童死亡率一直非常低。让我们所有人度过童年的技术是卫生设施、疫苗和抗生素。我敢肯定，强制性麻疹疫苗对美国儿童生存的贡献将超过 OpenAI 迄今为止花费数十亿美元所取得的成就。无论如何，我认为奥特曼实际上所做的是雇用一名保姆。或者考虑埃隆·马斯克向我们讲述未来的人形机器人仆人。我有一个机器人仆人。实际上有几个：洗碗机、洗衣机和烘干机。它们的移动性不太好，但它们为我节省了大量的劳动力。我的冰箱是 90 年代的，我的微波炉也不是很年轻，这两件事对我来说都是非凡的：让食物储存和烹饪变得简单，无需人工智能的参与。人工智能似乎无法在这些机器已经建立的基础上做出太多改进，特别是因为我的“愚蠢”技术已经 20 多年不需要更新了。省钱对我来说也很有价值。那些告诉我们人工智能将主宰我们的未来并取代我们工作的人正是希望这会成为现实的人。他们希望如此，因为这让他们感到自己很重要，或者因为他们想成为亿万富翁，或者因为他们根本不理解别人。我认为最后一点被低估了。如果你要为我提供一个机器人仆人，我有一个非常明确的标准：它至少要像我的洗碗机一样物有所值。在我们的生活中，有些地方的效率并不理想。普通人不会像断了头的鸡一样到处乱跑，试图让生活的每一个部分实现自动化。事实上，在我们的生活中，有些地方的效率并不理想。假期计划有时被认为是人工智能可以让我们的生活变得更轻松的地方。至少对我来说，计划假期本身就是一种乐趣。它使我能够浏览有关某个地方的信息，考虑什么可能有趣，并想象自己正在做这件事。如果我有朋友以前去过那个地方，我就有借口和他们交谈，听取他们的推荐。随着假期日期的临近，整个过程加剧了我的期待。但如果我想外包，我已经可以这样做了——那就是游轮和主题公园的用途是什么。法学硕士充其量只是一种企业技术，可以使某些类型的数据组织更容易，或者编码速度更快。这与大多数人的生活几乎无关。闲聊代码是许多技术人员喜欢的一种爱好，而我们其他人根本不关心。让编写代码变得更容易并不会改变我不想编写代码的事实。我还有其他爱好！法学硕士在大多数普通人的生活中的实际用途是作弊。对于成年人来说，它是查找信息——法学硕士正在取代谷歌搜索。一段时间以来，谷歌一直在降低其搜索项目的质量，而且结果越来越糟。这为另一种选择打开了大门，法学硕士也迈出了一步。我不知道这种情况会持续多久——法学硕士本身在某些时候会需要资金，而他们经常不准确（有时是抄袭）的结果正在扼杀他们赖以生成信息的网站。当然，点击进入高质量的产品效率较低，但是您还打算如何继续让人们生成高质量的信息呢？没有人解决这个问题。音乐家并不是在削弱创造力——他们是享受创作音乐的人。有时，低效率是一种负担。以股票市场为例。它仅在特定时间开放，并且仅在一周中的特定日期开放。这意味着在恐慌期间，存在一个人为的边界，让人们有时间冷静下来。这是有效的；这是个股有时会在歇斯底里期间停牌的原因之一。现在考虑一下加密货币，它 24/7/365 全天候开放：没有办法暂停恐慌。加密货币崩溃如此巨大和如此迅速的原因之一是因为没有断路器可以跳闸，也没有交易中断来让交易者重新组合。事实上，由于市场没有关闭，许多人根本无法入睡，这一事实可以说加剧了加密货币恐慌。消费者人工智能还有其他奇怪的方面。以人工智能音乐应用程序为例，它的理念是世界上有人想要创作音乐，但只是没有花时间学习如何演奏乐器。这样的人恐怕很少吧！音乐家并不是在削弱创造力——他们是喜欢创作音乐的人。我们其他人只是享受聆听，这本身就是目的。人工智能音乐最有用的地方是那些想要弄清楚如何让自己进入 Spotify 播放列表、积累流量并赚钱的人——也就是说，骗子。同样，自助出版市场充斥着人工智能的废品，不是因为人们拼命地试图表达自己，而是因为很容易欺骗其他人在亚马逊上购买废品。被骗的不仅仅是普通读者，《害羞女孩》的丑闻就证明了这一点，这本愚弄了阿歇特的小说现已撤回。对于大多数人来说，最终的结果是这些人工智能工具让他们更难接触到其他人创作的艺术作品。当然，对于艺术家来说，最终的结果是谋生更加困难。马克·扎克伯格的元乌托邦曾经长出腿吗？所有这些试图打造下一个产品来统治世界的神童怎么没有想到这一点？我认为答案很简单。他们和正常人没有太多共同点，也没有过多思考过正常人的生活是什么样的，正常人看重什么。相反，他们一直在做的是对自己的供应感到兴奋——听 VC 播客，担心自己是否能够跟上 AI 代理的步伐，否则就会变得越来越脱离现实。我怀疑这就是我们最终得到 NFT、虚拟宇宙和笨重的 VR/AR 耳机的原因。这些东西只吸引一小部分人，他们在风险投资和想成为科技企业家的领域中所占比例过高。硅谷的炒作周期对这些事情产生了巨大的影响，我想我们都知道结果如何。您最后一次听说“无聊猿”、“加密猫”或任何其他短暂席卷全国的新奇事物是什么时候？这些新奇事物是否会像我们所承诺的那样，转化为艺术家、音乐家和其他创作者真正、持久的收入来源？您最后一次看到有人佩戴 Apple 耳机是什么时候？马克·扎克伯格的元乌托邦曾经长出腿吗？听着，我们都对马克·安德森缺乏内省的牺牲感到有点开心——但这正是硅谷不断试图将他们绝对不想要的未来强加给消费者的原因。一个无法自我反省的风险投资家永远不会注意到，他对消费主义未来的押注每次都以完全相同的方式失败。那个 VC 没有注意到，实际上也没有注意到，他的经历并不代表普通人想要或需要的东西。“他们来了“事实上，在我挑剔安德森的同时，我想指出那次采访中的一些内容，但并没有走红。它发生在致命的自省引言之后，但我认为它触及了硅谷当前文化核心的真正腐烂之处。在其中，安德森提到了迷幻剂，说他正在与播客安德鲁·胡伯曼讨论迷幻剂。“我正在描述这一点这是我们在硅谷看到的现象，那里的人受到压力，感到焦虑或其他什么，有人告诉他们迷幻药，他们就尝试一下，”安德森说。“而他们从另一端出来时就像一个改变了的人。他们会更加平静地出来，但随后他们往往会退出自己的公司。”在安德森的讲述中，胡伯曼认为这些人可能会更快乐，生活也更好。安德森说，“是的，但他们的公司正在失败。”傲慢的企业家（以及需要他们的风险投资家）只占人口的一小部分。我们大多数人宁愿快乐，也不愿试图建立一家接管世界的公司——牺牲我们的大部分人。清醒的时间、我们的爱好，以及我们在这个过程中的许多关系，塑造未来的真正方法可能不是向消费者发号施令，而是向人们提供他们真正想要的东西。关注这个故事中的主题和作者，在您的个性化主页源中查看更多类似内容并接收电子邮件更新。

Original Summary

  1. One of the most mortifying things about knowing a lot of techies is listening to them tell me excitedly about some very important discovery that they believe they have made. Recently, I ran into an acquaintance of mine, who began talking my ear off about an amazing discovery he’d made with LLMs. Knowledge, it turns out, is structured into language! You could put one word into ChatGPT and it might understand what you wanted, or make up a word and see if it understood what you meant! These amazing new tools have revealed that the English corpus contains so much about its speakers!He concluded that LLMs are a discovery on par with writing.Regular humans hit on this idea about a century ago; my most generous interpretation of what he was telling me was that he’d hit on a kind of naive, confused version of Structuralism; Saussure via a game of telephone. (There has been recent work on a similar point, which argues that one needs to understand LLMs via literary theory, but it starts with Saussure.) I tried to get out of the conversation as quickly as I could, not least because he seemed frustrated that I didn’t see things exactly as he did — a new behavior and likely a symptom of LLM overuse.There is a certain amount of hubris required to throw oneself at an unsolved problem. But elsewhere, that hubris is a liability.Not every discovery that’s new to you is actually new. For instance, there’s Elon Musk marvelling at the complexity of hands; I could point to a variety of disciplines for which this is 101-level stuff: artists, who have to figure out how to draw them; surgeons, who have to figure out how to operate on them; musicians and magicians, who rely on extremely fine motor skill to produce their work; neuroscientists and psychologists, who doubtless encountered the cortical homunculus early in their careers. Or Palmer Luckey claiming that “no one has done a postmortem” on the One Laptop Per Child computing project — because he didn’t know there’s a whole book about it called The Charisma Machine.At its most absurd nadir, one is reminded of Juicero, a company that sold a $400 juicer that did the same work as squeezing its proprietary juice packs with one’s bare hands.Look, discovering something that’s new to you is exciting — ask anyone who listened to me yell about the joys of European (higher-fat) butter — but you can’t take for granted that something that’s new to you is new to everyone. These things have in common a certain incuriosity that I have found endemic among a certain kind of tech enthusiast, particularly the ones who are most interested in startups and entrepreneurship. Perhaps they have been so siloed that they did not realize their “discovery” was well -known elsewhere, or perhaps their self-conception is that they are the smartest, and if they don’t know something, no one knows it.There is a certain amount of hubris required to throw oneself at an unsolved problem — you have to believe you can solve it. But elsewhere, that hubris is a liability. It leads you to do weird things, like announce that Freud invented introspection and that it is a bonus that you simply do not engage in it.Within recent memory, people who made software and hardware understood their job was to serve their customerWhen I think I have observed something important, my first impulse is to go to a library, or Wikipedia, or a person who I think may be knowledgeable, and see what else has been observed. For instance, when I had a concussion, I wanted to see if anyone else had written about what it was like to recover — the dry medical descriptions did very little for me. When I couldn’t easily find an account, I wrote my own. I still receive emails about it, years later, from people who are doing the same search I did, following their own concussions. But doing something like this requires you to take for granted that other people are smart, that smart people have always existed, and that very little in the human experience is new. That requires, you know, intellectual humility — and a willingness to think about other people’s experiences.While this particular kind of hubris makes people crashing bores, it’s not just an annoying personal trait. It seems to have seeped into the professional side of Silicon Valley as well.Within recent memory, people who made software and hardware understood their job was to serve their customer. It was to identify a need, and then fill it. But at some point following the financial crisis, would-be entrepreneurs got it into their heads that their job was to invent the future, and consumers’ job was to go along with that invented future. My guess is that they’re aping what they thought Steve Jobs was doing when he, for instance, got rid of the optical drives on the MacBook Air.But Steve Jobs, famously, failed at inventing the future in the 1980s and got booted from Apple. We all know how things changed when he came back. But the iMac, the iPod, the iPhone were built with a need in mind. The iMac won because it was easy to use. The iPod was easier to take with you than a CD player and a stack of CDs. (It also was a way to play the MP3s you might have illegally downloaded.) The iPhone had the App Store, which expanded its utility well beyond any other mobile device.At some point, our Silicon Valley overlords forgot that in order for their vision of the future to be adopted, people had to want it.Some of this was luck — introducing the right product at the right time. But each product offered consumers a distinct value proposition. Sure, early adopters jumped on each of these things because they were cool, but the uncool masses don’t care about that. They’ll buy something if it improves their life in a distinct way.In the place of problem-solving technology, companies have jumped on successive bandwagons like NFTs, the metaverse, and large language models. What these all have in common is that they are not built to really solve a market problem. They are built to make VCs and companies rich. NFTs, like crypto, let VCs quickly unload investments with abbreviated lockup periods. The metaverse promised to enrich companies like Facebook by having people move all their socializing online, where it could be surveilled and monetized. In addition, Facebook’s metaverse required the purchase of hardware, which would then need regular upgrades.At some point, our Silicon Valley overlords forgot that in order for their vision of the future to be adopted, people had to want it. That’s why NFTs, the metaverse, and the Oculus and Vision Pro never really found their customer base. AI is, admittedly, more useful — it’s good for organizing large swaths of data, for instance. LLMs have had widespread consumer adoption, at least as long as they remain free. But there is only really one customer for LLMs that can justify the massive cash incineration process that was required to build them: the US government.There can only be a few winners on government contracts, though. So we are now treated to the spectacle of watching AI companies scramble. OpenAI is perhaps the funniest, because it is attempting to position itself as a consumer product.The people who tell us that AI will dominate our future and take our jobs are the people who are hoping that will be true.Consider Sam Altman telling the world that he needed ChatGPT to tell him how to raise a baby. You exist. I exist. Our parents did not have LLMs, or even AI, and yet somehow we survived our childhoods, as did almost everyone else we knew growing up because childhood death rates in the US have been extraordinarily low — compared to most of the rest of human history — for decades. The technologies that allowed us all to survive our childhoods were sanitation, vaccines, and antibiotics. I would put money down that a mandatory measles vaccine will do more for the survival of American children than anything OpenAI has accomplished with all of its billions of dollars to date.In any event, I presume what Altman actually did was hire a nanny.Or consider Elon Musk telling us about our future humanoid robot servants. I have a robot servant. Several, actually: a dishwasher, a washer for my clothing, and a dryer. They aren’t very mobile, and yet they have saved me tremendous labor. My fridge is from the ’90s, and my microwave isn’t much younger, and both of those things have been remarkable in what they have done for me: made food storage and cooking easy, without AI involvement. It doesn’t seem like there’s much AI can do to improve things over the baseline that these machines have already established, especially since my “dumb” technology hasn’t required an update in more than 20 years. Saving money is valuable to me, too.The people who tell us that AI will dominate our future and take our jobs are the people who are hoping that will be true. They may be hoping this because it makes them feel important, or because they want to be billionaires, or because they simply do not understand other people. I think that final point is underestimated. If you are going to provide me with a robot servant, I have a very clear bar: It’s gotta be at least as much bang for my buck as my dishwasher.There are places in our lives where efficiency isn’t desirableNormal people aren’t running around like chickens with their heads cut off, trying to automate every single part of their lives. Indeed, there are places in our lives where efficiency isn’t desirable. Vacation planning is sometimes suggested as a place AI can make our lives easier. For me, at least, planning the vacation is a pleasure in and of itself; it allows me to browse information about a place, consider what might be fun, and imagine myself doing it. If I have friends who have been to that place before, it gives me an excuse to talk to them, getting their recommendations. The entire process sharpens the anticipation I feel as the date for the vacation approaches. But if I wish to outsource that, I can do so already — that’s what cruise ships and theme parks are for.LLMs are, at best, an enterprise technology that may make certain kinds of data organization easier, or coding faster. This has almost nothing to do with most people’s lives. Dinking around with code is a hobby many tech people enjoy and one the rest of us simply don’t care about. Making it easier to write code doesn’t change that I don’t want to write code. I have other hobbies!The actual use for LLMs in most normal people’s lives is cheating on schoolwork. For adults, it’s looking up information — LLMs are in the process of supplanting Google Search. Google had been degrading its search project for some time, and the results just kept getting worse. This opened the door for an alternative, and the LLMs stepped through. How long that will last, I don’t know — the LLMs themselves will require money at some point and their frequently inaccurate (and sometimes plagiarized) results are killing the websites they rely on to generate information. Sure, it’s more inefficient to click through to a high-quality product, but how else do you plan to continue to have people generate high-quality information? No one has solved this problem.Musicians aren’t bogarting creativity — they are people who enjoy making musicSometimes inefficiency is load-bearing. Take, for instance, the stock market. It is only open during certain hours, and only during certain days of the week. That means that during a panic, there is an artificial boundary that gives people time to calm down. This is effective; it’s one of the reasons that individual stocks sometimes undergo a trading halt during periods of hysteria. Now consider crypto, which is open for business 24/7/365: There is no way to pause a panic. One of the reasons the crashes in crypto are so huge and so fast is because there is no breaker to trip and no break in trading to allow traders to regroup. In fact, crypto panics are arguably exacerbated by the fact that many people literally cannot sleep because the market does not close.There are other ways in which consumer AI is weird. Take the AI music apps, which are predicated on the idea that there are people in the world who want to make music but simply haven’t taken the time to learn how to play an instrument. There are likely very few of those people! Musicians aren’t bogarting creativity — they are people who enjoy making music. The rest of us just enjoy listening, which is an end in itself.The place where AI music is most useful is for people who want to figure out how to get themselves onto Spotify playlists, accrue streams, and make money — that is to say, scammers. Similarly, the self-publishing market is rampant with AI slop, not because people are desperately trying to express themselves, but because it is easy to trick other people into buying slop on Amazon. And it’s not just the casual readers who get swindled, as demonstrated by the scandal around Shy Girl, the now-withdrawn novel that fooled Hachette. The end result for most people is that these AI tools make it harder for them to access art made by other people. And the end result for artists, of course, is that it’s harder to make a living.Did Mark Zuckerberg’s Meta utopia ever develop legs?How is it that all these wunderkinds trying to build the next product to take over the world haven’t thought about this? I think the answer is simple. They do not have much in common with normal people, and haven’t thought much about what normal people’s lives are like, or what normal people value. What they have been doing instead is getting high on their own supply — listening to VC podcasts, freaking themselves out about whether they’ll be able to keep up with AI agents, and otherwise getting increasingly more detached from reality.I suspect this is how we wound up with NFTs, the metaverse, and the clunky VR/AR headsets. These are things that appeal to a very narrow set of people who are overrepresented in the VC and wannabe-tech-entrepreneur spaces. The Silicon Valley hype cycle worked overtime for those things, and I think we all know how this turned out. When was the last time you heard about a Bored Ape, or a Crypto Kitty, or any of the other novelties that briefly swept the nation? Did those novelties translate into a real, durable income stream for artists, musicians, and other creators, as we were promised? When was the last time you saw someone wearing Apple’s headset? Did Mark Zuckerberg’s Meta utopia ever develop legs?Look, we all had a bit of fun at Marc Andreessen’s expense about his lack of introspection — but this is precisely the reason Silicon Valley keeps trying to force futures on consumers that they emphatically don’t want. A VC who is incapable of self-reflection will never notice that his bets on the future of consumerism are failing in exactly the same way every time. That VC hasn’t noticed, and indeed can’t notice, that his experience isn’t representative of what the ordinary person wants or needs.“They come out much more at peace, but then they tend to quit their companies.”Actually, while I’m picking on Andreessen, I want to point to a bit of that interview that didn’t go viral. It occurs right after the fatal introspection quote, but I think it gets to the real rot at the heart of Silicon Valley’s current culture. In it, Andreessen mentions psychedelics, saying he was discussing them with podcaster Andrew Huberman. “I was describing this phenomenon we see in Silicon Valley, where there are these guys who get under pressure, and they feel anxious or whatever, and someone tells them about psychedelics, and they try it,” Andreessen says. “And they kind of come out the other end as a changed person. They come out much more at peace, but then they tend to quit their companies.”In Andreessen’s telling, Huberman suggests that these people may be happier, and better off. And Andreessen says, “Yeah, but their company is failing.”The hubristic entrepreneurs (and the VCs who need them) are a relatively small slice of the population. The majority of us would much rather be happy than try to found a company that takes over the world — sacrificing the majority of our waking hours, our hobbies, and likely many of our relationships in the process. It may be the case that the real way to shape the future isn’t to dictate it to consumers. It is simpler just to give people things they actually want.Follow topics and authors from this story to see more like this in your personalized homepage feed and to receive email updates.Elizabeth Lopatto

irq-1 2026/04/27 22:28
> 我怀疑这就是我们最终得到 NFT、元宇宙和笨重的 VR/AR 耳机的原因。

VR/AR 是因为新技术让我们能够做一些我们尚未弄清楚的事情。它不是由未来愿景驱动的，而是由硬件进步驱动的。

虚拟宇宙是扎克伯格大肆宣传的一个老想法，因为 Facebook 变得不再酷了。此举的目的是为了保持公司的相关性，并让它改变 Facebook 的名称。

NFT 是一种将货币转移到比特币等数字世界的尝试。理想主义还是粗俗的机会主义尚有争议，但破碎的科技理念并不是什么新鲜事。

> I suspect this is how we wound up with NFTs, the metaverse, and the clunky VR/AR headsets.

VR/AR is because new tech allows us to do something... that we haven't figured out yet. It's not driven by visions of the future but by hardware advances.

The metaverse was an old idea that Zuckerberg hyped because Facebook became un-cool. It was meant to keep the company relevant and let it change the name away from Facebook.

NFTs was an attempt to copy moneys move to the digital world like bitcoin. Whether idealistic or crass opportunism is debatable, but broken tech ideas are nothing new.

在 HN 中查看
pstuart 2026/04/28 01:38
NFT 没有任何存在的理由，除了提供洗钱机会和骗取现金之外。加密货币有很多让人不喜欢的地方，其中最重要的是 NFT。
NFTs had no valid reasons to exist, other than to provide money laundering opportunities and to con rubes out of their cash. There's plenty to not like about crypto and at the top of that list is NFTs.
在 HN 中查看
jfengel 2026/04/28 02:50
一些艺术家一开始就喜欢 NFT 合约可以让他们保留部分权利的想法。这样，如果作品后来卖了一大笔钱，他们就可以从中赚到一些钱。

但他们很快就幻灭了。整个空间立即充满了骗子出售的垃圾艺术品。当然，开发人员知道会发生这种情况，但艺术家并不总是有同样的直觉，因为一种技术的最糟糕的使用方式将压倒所有其他技术。

Some artists right at the beginning liked the idea that an NFT contract could let them keep some fraction of the rights. That way, if the work later sold for a large sum, they could earn some money off of it.

But they were quickly disillusioned. The space instantly filled with crap art sold by scammers. Developers, of course, knew that would happen, but artists don't always have that same instinct for the way the worst possible use of a technology will overwhelm all others.

在 HN 中查看
georgemcbay 2026/04/28 07:19
> 一些艺术家一开始就喜欢 NFT 合约可以让他们保留部分权利的想法。

NFT 从来没有提供任何普通纸质合约无法提供的东西。

它们既没有提供针对复制的增强的实际保护，也没有提供任何增强的知识产权法律保护。

任何认为 NFT 除了短暂炒作之外还没有取得任何成就的艺术家都被误导了。

> Some artists right at the beginning liked the idea that an NFT contract could let them keep some fraction of the rights.

NFTs never provided a single thing that a normal paper contract couldn't.

They provided neither enhanced practical protections vs copying nor any enhanced intellectual property legal protections.

Any artists who thought NFTs accomplished anything at all other than a brief wave of hype were misinformed.

Ekaros 2026/04/28 16:08
我从来不知道区块链除了分发之外还能做什么更好。甚至还引发了在很多地方复制数据的效率问题。

纠正数据也始终是一个有趣的问题。你的钥匙被偷了，现在你的房子不再是你的了，因为记录是不可变的。哦，如果有人说它不是（用新记录替换它），它实际上并不是不可变的？又是什么意思呢...

I never got what would blockchain do better except maybe distribution. And even there raise questions of efficiency of having data copied in a lot of places.

Correcting data is also always fun question. Your keys are stolen, now your house is not yours anymore as record is immutable. Oh it is not actually immutable if someone says it is not (replace it with new record )? What was the point again...

marbro 2026/04/27 22:53 deleted

在 HN 中查看
jerojero 2026/04/27 23:18
硅谷人的钱比他们的时间多。因此，被称为“节省时间”或“提高生产力”的解决方案确实引起了他们的共鸣。

然而，我想说的是，大多数人实际上拥有的时间比他们拥有的金钱还要多。因此，人们做出了一些努力，将法学硕士和生成式人工智能转变为注意力经济，但它还不具有成本效益，并且内容创作者强烈反对，只要有空间来托管，他们就非常愿意免费制作内容。

我喜欢法学硕士让我更容易进行编程，但我也觉得我之前所做的事情......很好。我有一种感觉，科技领域的人们认为总会有“尚未发现”的新创新软件，因此法学硕士带来的生产力提升将带来一个无限创造力的时代。我确信我们将会看到更多更好的视频游戏和更多更好的软件。但同时，我担心我们作为人们从软件中获得的效用可能会达到一个平台期，相反，我们只是试图一遍又一遍地重新发明轮子，进行微小的改进。

最终，AGI 世界会是什么样子？对我来说，我想花更多的时间和我的朋友在一起，我觉得我已经失去了生产力机器。

老实说，我每天接触的最有趣的人都是悠闲的人，他们中的很多人暂时失业，或者从事任何能让他们过得去的工作，这就是通用人工智能的承诺，但与此同时……以我们已经可以聚集但选择不这样做的生产力来实现这样一个世界可能并不难。因此，我对 AGI 所带来的时间承诺有点怀疑。

People in Silicon Valley have more money than they have time. So solutions that are framed as "time savers" or "productivity enhancers" really resonate with them.

However, the bulk of people I'd say actually have more time than they do money. So there was a bit of an effort to turn LLMs and generative AI into attention economy, it's not cost effective yet and there's a big push against it from content creators who are more than willing to make content for free so long as they are given a space to host it.

I like that the LLMs make it easier for me to do programming, but I also felt like what I was doing before was... fine. I kind of get a feeling that people in the tech space think there's always going to be new innovative software that's sort of "not yet discovered" and so this productivity gain that LLMs bring is going to bring an era of unbridled creativity. And I definitely think we're going to be seeing more and better video-games and more and better software. But also, I'm afraid that the utility we as people get from software might be reaching a plateau and instead we are just trying to re-invent the wheel over and over with marginal improvements.

Ultimately, what does an AGI world would even look like? For me, I would like to spend more time with my friends whom I feel I've lost to the productivity machine.

To be honest, the most fun people I interact with on a daily basis are laid-back people, a lot of them in temporary unemployment or in whatever jobs gets them by, and that's kind of the promise of AGI but at the same time... it might not be that hard to achieve such a world with the kind of productivity we can already muster and have chosen not to. So I'm a little bit skeptical in the promise of time that AGI supposedly will bring.

ryandrake 2026/04/28 00:36
很棒的评论。我想补充一点，百万富翁科技高管很大程度上是那些梦想着这些法学硕士产品的人，他们与“普通人”的互动程度可能为零。他们有私人助理做所有这些事情。你认为祖克会自己买杂货还是马斯克会自己买卡其裤？没办法，他们与现实世界的全部交互都是通过助手，所以难怪他们的产品愿景仅限于“像我的助手一样：听话、有活力、积极向上的实习生，认同并执行我的每一句话”。
Great comment. I'd add that millionaire tech executives are largely the ones dreaming up these LLM products, and their level of interaction with "ordinary people" is probably nil. They have personal assistants doing all that stuff. Do you think Zucc shops for his own groceries or Musk buys his own Khakis? No way, their entire interaction with the real world is through assistants, so it's no wonder that their product vision is limited to "something just like my assistant: obedient, energetic, positive intern who agrees with and executes my every word".

ryandrake 2026/04/28 00:49
> 在最近的记忆中，制造软件和硬件的人明白他们的工作是服务客户。这是为了确定需求，然后满足它。但在金融危机之后的某个时刻，潜在的企业家开始意识到，他们的工作就是创造未来，而消费者的工作就是顺应所创造的未来。

我喜欢这句话。确实引起了共鸣。我想不出过去（比如 5 年）发明的主要技术产品能够真正满足我的需求。至少十年来，我对电脑、手机或 Cloud-Thinggy 并没有真正感到兴奋。几年过去了，“看！稍微好一点的相机和表情符号！”和“速度较慢的应用程序，执行的操作较少，但 看起来很简约！”当然还有“现在有了人工智能！”加上几十个我永远不会使用的新网站和流媒体服务，因为我不明白为什么我会使用。硅谷只是“这里有一些社交媒体和一堆薄笔记本电脑。习惯就好。”

> Within recent memory, people who made software and hardware understood their job was to serve their customer. It was to identify a need, and then fill it. But at some point following the financial crisis, would-be entrepreneurs got it into their heads that their job was to invent the future, and consumers’ job was to go along with that invented future.

I love this quote. It really resonates. I can't think of a major technology product invented in the last, say 5 years, that actually served to fulfill a need that I had. I haven't really been excited about a computer or phone or Cloud-Thinggy for at least a decade. It's just been years of "Look! Slightly better camera and emojis!" and "Slower applications that do less but look so minimal!" and of course "Now with AI!" Plus a dozens of new web sites and streaming services that I'll just never use because I don't understand why I would. Silicon Valley is just "Here's some social media and a bunch of thin laptops. Get used to it."

Dig1t 2026/04/28 09:58
>有人说：“给顾客他们想要的东西。”但这不是我的方法。我们的工作是在他们这样做之前弄清楚他们想要什么。我记得亨利·福特曾经说过，“如果我问顾客他们想要什么，他们会告诉我，‘一匹更快的马！’”人们不知道他们想要什么，直到你向他们展示。

我认为我们都只是想想出一些很酷的东西并向人们展示，看看他们是否想要它。

>Some people say, "Give the customers what they want." But that's not my approach. Our job is to figure out what they're going to want before they do. I think Henry Ford once said, "If I'd asked customers what they wanted, they would have told me, 'A faster horse!'" People don't know what they want until you show it to them.

I think we’re all just trying to come up with cool things and show it to people to see if they want it.

在 HN 中查看
noir_lord 2026/04/27 22:02
这真是一篇写得很好的文章。

我已经厌倦了人工智能并听说它，以至于我开始使用 ublock 起源自定义过滤器在我经常访问的网站（包括 HN）上摧毁它。

我不知道它是否会辜负炒作，但如果确实如此，我会以其他方式听到它，直到他们解决一个我没有或不关心的问题，并摧毁我确实关心的事情，所以我只想 忽略他们。

That's a really well written article.

I've become so tired of AI and hearing about it that I've started using ublock origin custom filters to nuke it on sites I frequent (including HN).

I don't know if it'll live up to the hype but if it does I'll hear about it other ways til then they are solving a problem I don't have or care about and doing it my destroying things I do care about so I'm just going to ignore them.

noir_lord 2026/04/27 22:06
最原始的方式可能（通过自定义过滤器） - 当它停止工作时，我可能只会做一个浏览器插件。

    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/AI/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/AI/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/claude/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/claudeI/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/llm/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/llm/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/vibecode/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/vibecode/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/agi/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/agi/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/deep learning/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/deep learning/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/agent/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/agent/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/TPU/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/TPU/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/GPT/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/GPT/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/DeepSeek/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/DeepSeak/i)) + tr
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/Anthropic/i))
    news.ycombinator.com##tr.athing:has(a:has-text(/Anthropic/i)) + tr
通过消除（对我来说）噪音并增加信号，它使 HN 更像是我首先欣赏的 HN 。

altmanaltman 2026/04/28 01:47
LLM 是没人想要的东西，但 ChatGPT 仍然被世界上 10% 的人口作为活跃用户使用？这两个句子怎么能同时为真呢？
LLMs are something nobody wants but still ChatGPT is used by 10% of the world's population as active users? How can both the sentences be true at the same time?
在 HN 中查看
protimewaster 2026/04/28 04:20
我不觉得很难相信这两者都是真的。

我不想开车去上班，但我还是开车去上班，因为没有公共交通可以到达我的工作地点。

我不想使用 Gmail 作为工作电子邮件，但工作需要我使用 Gmail，所以我使用它。

有些人使用 ChatGPT 只是因为有人说：“你需要知道如何使用人工智能来保持竞争力。”

我并不是说我同意或不同意这种观点，但我坚信这两句话可以同时成立。

I don't find it hard to believe that both can be true.

I don't want to have to commute to work by driving, but I do anyway, because there's no public transit to my work.

I don't want to have to use Gmail for my work email, but that's what work wants me to use, so I use it.

Some number of people are using ChatGPT just because somebody said, "You need to know how to use AI to stay competitive."

I'm not saying I agree or disagree with the sentiment, but I firmly believe both sentences can be true at the same time.

在 HN 中查看
altmanaltman 2026/04/28 12:51
哦，所以他们被迫使用人工智能，并且实际上讨厌它，而不是该产品的真正用户。

但即使在这种情况下，这是否意味着人们确实“想要”达到每天对 8 亿人这样做的程度？

因此，即使在这种情况下（如果我们接受它，甚至将其推向极端），没有人希望人工智能实际上仍然是错误的。

Oh so they're being forced to use AI and actually hate it instead of being genuine users of the product.

But even in this case, it does mean people do "want" it to a level where they are doing this to 800 million people everyday?

So even in that case (if we accept it and even take it to the extreme), nobody wants AI is still factually wrong.

在 HN 中查看
shengpuerh 2026/04/28 05:50
广告就是让你想要一些你不需要的东西（例如，因为害怕被抛在后面）。如果硅谷不强迫人们适应或落后，这种需求还会存在吗？我认为这篇文章使用“想要”有点松散，因为人们确实 想 使用人工智能并做事，但我猜想，在人工智能用户中，主观生活满意度并不总是更高——尤其是当一个人觉得有必要这样做时。
Advertising is all about making you want something you don't need (e.g. for fear of being left behind). Would the want exist without silicon valley shoving it down people's throats by telling them to adapt or get left behind? I think the article uses "want" kind of loosely, because people do want to use AI and do, but I would guess that among AI users, subjective life satisfaction is is not always higher - especially if one feels compelled to do it.
在 HN 中查看
altmanaltman 2026/04/28 12:54
我不明白？是的，广告可能具有操纵性，但您肯定不是说硅谷正在强迫 8 亿人积极使用 ChatGPT？即使情况确实如此，为什么我们会看到如此多的竞争对手也拥有大量的活跃用户？

我认为建模一个所有这些活跃用户都被操纵的世界要复杂得多。一个更简单的解释是，作者正在迎合公众的某一部分，并通过这个镜头来建立整个世界的观点。

这就是为什么当记者开始为“所有人”说话时，即使数据表明他们是错误的，也要始终保持怀疑态度，这一点很重要。

I don't understand? Yeah advertising can be manipulative but you surely arent saying Silicon Valley is forcing 800 million people actively to use ChatGPT? Even if thats the case, why are we seeing so many competitors that are also seeing massive numbers of active users?

I think its much more complex to model a world where all these active users are being manipulated etc. A much simpler explanation is that the author is pandering to a certain section of the public and basing the entire world's view through that lens.

This is why its important to always be skeptical when journalists start speaking for "everyone" even in the face of data showing they are wrong.

在 HN 中查看
binary132 2026/04/28 08:56
我认为情况并非如此，而更像是一群人盲目地追逐大额资金，在一个巨大的风险投资漩涡中上升，而经济的其他部分则慢慢腐烂。 换句话说，没有思想，没有领导力，大多只是炒作、泡沫和追逐趋势。
I think it’s less like that and more like a bunch of people blindly chasing big line go up in a big venture capital vortex while the rest of the economy slowly rots. In other words, no thought, no leadership, mostly just hype, bubbles, and trend chasing.
在 HN 中查看
SpicyLemonZest 2026/04/27 22:36
我发现很难摆脱这样的印象：作者只是认为硅谷充满了书呆子，并想把他们塞进储物柜里。我现在已经不是埃隆·马斯克的粉丝了，但你怎么能如此不好奇，以至于你观看他解释机器人手的实际障碍的视频，并反驳说这是“101级的东西”，因为其他领域的其他人也知道手很复杂？

可以肯定的是，整篇文章中都散布着一些真正的观点。人工智能污染正在污染公共领域，这是一个问题。但是，就像这样：

> 为什么所有这些试图开发下一个产品来统治世界的神童都没有考虑到这一点？我认为答案很简单。他们和正常人没有太多共同点，也没有过多思考过正常人的生活是什么样的，正常人看重什么。相反，他们一直在做的是对自己的供应感到兴奋——听风险投资播客，担心自己是否能够跟上人工智能代理的步伐，否则就会越来越脱离现实。

这不是一个认为技术人员或他们的利益值得尊重的人所写的段落。

I'm finding it really hard to escape the impression that the author just thinks Silicon Valley is full of nerds and wants to stuff them in lockers. I'm far from an Elon Musk fanboy these days, but how can you be so incurious that you watch a video of him explaining the practical obstacles to robotic hands, and retort that this is "101-level stuff" because other people in other fields also know hands are complex?

There's real points scattered throughout the article, to be sure. It's a problem that AI slop is polluting the commons. But, like, this:

> How is it that all these wunderkinds trying to build the next product to take over the world haven’t thought about this? I think the answer is simple. They do not have much in common with normal people, and haven’t thought much about what normal people’s lives are like, or what normal people value. What they have been doing instead is getting high on their own supply — listening to VC podcasts, freaking themselves out about whether they’ll be able to keep up with AI agents, and otherwise getting increasingly more detached from reality.

is not a paragraph written by someone who feels that techies or their interests are worthy of respect.

在 HN 中查看
saltcured 2026/04/28 01:28
从你的开场白来看，我真的希望你能走向不同的方向......

我认为有趣的是，有多少所谓的“技术”人员实际上只是具有高风险/高回报心态的商业/投资人员。他们不是书呆子。这项技术只是他们收入和资本收益愿景的当代设定。

在另一个时代，他们梦想着出售电影票或控制航线。并不是因为他们关心电影制作或运输，而只是因为他们对专属市场垂涎欲滴。

由于科技、消费主义、媒体和广告的格式塔合并，我认为存在一种风险投资心态，他们认为他们可以定义下一个事物并告知公众他们的下一个渴望。

From your opening sentence, I really expected you to go in a different direction...

I think the funny thing is how many supposed "tech" people are nothing but business/investment people with a high risk/high reward mindsets. They are not the nerds. The tech is just a contemporary set piece for their visions of revenue and capital gains.

In another era, they'd be dreaming about selling movie tickets, or controlling shipping routes. Not because they care about film making, or transport, but simply because they salivate over the captive market.

Because of the gestalt merger of tech, consumerism, media, and advertising, I think there is a VC mindset that know thinks they can just define the Next Thing and inform the public of their next craving.

在 HN 中查看
SpicyLemonZest 2026/04/28 02:31
我只是认为这些都不正确。为什么一个不关心技术的商业/投资人士会关注大多数人不想要的听起来奇怪的东西？如果他们不在乎占领哪个市场，他们就会选择人们确实想要的众多听起来很正常的东西之一，或者可能成为一名宽客，直接从华尔街赚到钱。
I just don't think any of this is correct. Why would a business/investment person who doesn't care about tech focus on strange-sounding things that most people don't want? If they didn't care which market they capture, they'd pick one of the many normal-sounding things that people do want, or perhaps go become a quant and farm their money directly from Wall Street.
在 HN 中查看
saltcured 2026/04/28 03:55
请耐心听我按相反的顺序进行。这些类型不能成为宽客。这是一个不同的人，他有金钱的愿景，但也是一个有能力从事技术工作的书呆子。我说的是雇用程序员、工程师或宽客的风险投资人。

这些人认为自己有伟大的想法，对他们来说，无论是雇佣创意人员来制作艺术品、雇佣制造迷来制造半导体、雇佣软件迷来制造 SaaS，还是雇佣生物化学家来制造药品，都没有什么不同。对于他们和他们神奇的思维来说，这都是难以理解的平凡细节。在对这些事物进行了职业观察之后，我开始将抽象视为一种可以从美德转变为邪恶的东西。

我认为人工智能的梦想正在让一些这类人走向疯狂。正如另一个子线程中的某人所观察到的那样，它与他们与世界互动的方式产生了强烈的共鸣，委托了你并不真正理解的事情。但它也承诺了一种纯粹的资本主义扩张，他们的收入梦想可以不受劳动力限制的束缚。目前的争夺是试图将每一个商业梦想转变为使用这一新定位的梦想。

Bear with me while I go in reverse order. These types could not become quants. That is a different person who has the visions of money but is also a nerd capable of the technical work. I'm talking about the VC people who hire programmers, engineers, or quants.

These people think they have Big Ideas and to them it isn't really different whether they hire creatives to make art, manufacturing nerds to make semiconductors, software nerds to make SaaS, or biochemists to make pharmaceuticals. It's all inscrutable mundane details to them and their magical thinking. After a career observing such things, I've come to see abstraction as something that can cross from virtue into malignancy.

I think the AI dream is driving some of this type towards madness. As someone in another sub-thread observed, It resonates strongly with the way they engage with the world, with delegation of things you don't really understand. But it also promises a kind of pure capitalist scaling where their revenue dreams can be untethered from labor constraints. The current scramble is the attempt to pivot every business dream into one that uses this new set piece.

在 HN 中查看
Nasrudith 2026/04/28 03:48
我不一定同意这个论点，但风险投资模型在解释为什么商业/投资人员会这样做的方式上有点奇怪。

根据我的理解，“大多数人不关心的奇怪事情”可能会失败（事实上，可能会失败），但它们过去也曾是主要的彩票。当你有足够的钱来挥霍时，“购买每张乐透彩票”作为一种策略，会比采取“理性”方法（仅投资于那些你拥有强大基本面的领域）获得更好的回报。在这些领域进行多元化投资需要大量资金，因此当然并不适合所有人。迄今为止，科技公司在高度可扩展的业务方面拥有最好的记录。

他们只关心回报。如果这个大领域不是我们所知道的“科技”，而是基因工程，或者为了荒谬——字面上的魔法。如果它能以某种方式扩大规模，他们就会投入资金。

I don't necessarily agree with the thesis but the venture capital model is a bit weird in ways that explain why a business/investment person would do that.

From my understanding 'Strange things most people don't care about' may fail (and indeed, probably will fail) but they have also been major lottery tickets in the past. When you have enough money to throw around 'buying every lotto ticket' has a record of paying off better as a strategy than taking a 'rational' approach of only investing in those which you have strong fundamentals in. It takes a stupid amount of money to diversify in such fields so it isn't for everyone of course. Tech just has the best record for it so far with highly scaleable businesses.

They just care about the returns. If instead of what we know as 'tech' the big field was, say, genetic engineering or for the sake of absurdity - literal magic. If it somehow scaled better they would throw their money at that.

JohnFen 2026/04/27 23:07
他并不是在谈论这里的所有技术人员。他谈论的是一小部分：

> 某种技术爱好者，尤其是那些对初创企业和创业最感兴趣的人。

He's not talking about all tech people here. He's talking about a narrow subset:

> a certain kind of tech enthusiast, particularly the ones who are most interested in startups and entrepreneurship.

在 HN 中查看
SpicyLemonZest 2026/04/27 23:25
我确信她认为自己并不是在谈论所有技术人员，但她以一个关于她的熟人的轶事开始了这篇文章，描述了听他谈论自己的想法是如何“令人羞愧”，并直接将他与“普通人”（当然，“普通人”对瑞士语言学家费迪南德·德·索绪尔很熟悉）进行了对比。

我不想过度索引这一轶事，因为我认识到这是一种相当敌对的解释，我可以轻松地将其视为俏皮的玩笑 如果 文章的其余部分与这一观点一致。不过，有点不是。

I'm sure she thinks she's not talking about all tech people, but she began the article with an anecdote about an acquaintance of hers, describing how it was "mortifying" to listen to him talk about his ideas and directly contrasting him with a "regular human" (who of course would be familiar with Swiss linguist Ferdinand de Saussure).

I don't want to overindex on the one anecdote, because I recognize this is a pretty hostile interpretation, and I could just as easily read it as playful ribbing if the rest of the article were consistent with that perspective. It's kinda not, though.

jrflowers 2026/04/28 04:14
> 作者只是认为硅谷充满了书呆子

很长一段时间以来，这并不是硅谷的刻板印象。 HBO 有一整部喜剧，讲述了一个小镇上的一小群书呆子的故事，其中的其他角色都是“一夜赚了很多钱，然后宣称自己是上帝”的变体。

> the author just thinks Silicon Valley is full of nerds

That hasn’t been the stereotype for Silicon Valley in a long time. There was a whole HBO comedy show about a small group of nerds in a town where every other character is a variation of “made a bunch of money overnight and then declared themselves God”
